message.content:
You are an INDEPENDENT verifier adjudicating audit finding GitHub issue #5 for /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. You did NOT author it. TRUE_POSITIVE vs FALSE_POSITIVE; try HARD to refute. Run repros with /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python. READ-ONLY: no edits, no git, no gh.

FINDING (#5, stated severity MEDIUM): In src/assay_engine/methodology/firewalls.py:69-104 (used by confirm.py:80), Firewall B is a no-op in the degenerate case: DiscoverConfirmSplit.from_partition({"a"}, set()) constructs without error, and assert_confirm_only(set()) (or any empty evaluated_ids) passes vacuously — so confirm_unit_level can emit a verdict for a "held-out" test that touched zero held-out objects.

Independently verify: repro both (empty confirm partition construction; assert_confirm_only(set()) not raising). Consider counter-arguments (is an empty confirm partition ever legitimate? is requiring non-empty evaluated_ids the right place to enforce?). Assess MEDIUM severity.

Return EXACTLY:
VERDICT: TRUE_POSITIVE | FALSE_POSITIVE | PARTIAL
CONFIDENCE: high | medium | low
SEVERITY_ASSESSMENT: <...>
REASONING: <your own evidence>
FIX_GUIDANCE: <...>

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding independently. Let me start by reading the relevant files.

content.content:
1	"""The two firewalls, enforced as runtime primitives (ADR-0005, METHODOLOGY.md §2).
2	
3	Both separations are enforced in code and exercised by tests — never asserted only in prose.
4	
5	Firewall A — claim-blindness:
6	    The baseline must be built blind to external claims. :class:`ClaimBlindGuard` seals a
7	    claims source so that any attempt to read it while the guard is active raises
8	    :class:`FirewallViolation`. Baseline construction runs inside the guard; adjudication
9	    runs outside it.
10	
11	Firewall B — discover/confirm separation:
12	    The data used to discover a hypothesis must not be the data used to confirm it.
13	    :class:`DiscoverConfirmSplit` holds disjoint discovery and confirmation id sets and
14	    refuses to confirm on any id the discovery phase touched.
15	"""
16	
17	from __future__ import annotations
18	
19	from contextlib import contextmanager
20	from dataclasses import dataclass
21	from typing import Iterable, Iterator
22	
23	
24	class FirewallViolation(RuntimeError):
25	    """Raised when a methodological firewall is breached at runtime."""
26	
27	
28	class ClaimBlindGuard:
29	    """Firewall A. While sealed, ``access()`` raises — the baseline cannot read claims.
30	
31	    Usage::
32	
33	        guard = ClaimBlindGuard()
34	        with guard.sealed():
35	            corpus = parser.parse(source)      # baseline build — no claims reachable
36	            baseline = build_baseline(corpus, claim_guard=guard)
37	        claims = guard.access(claims_source)    # adjudication — guard released
38	
39	    The baseline builder calls ``guard.access(...)`` defensively; if it ever does so inside
40	    the sealed block, the violation surfaces immediately instead of silently contaminating
41	    the reference.
42	    """
43	
44	    def __init__(self) -> None:
45	        self._sealed = False
46	
47	    @property
48	    def is_sealed(self) -> bool:
49	        return self._sealed
50	
51	    @contextmanager
52	    def sealed(self) -> Iterator["ClaimBlindGuard"]:
53	        prior = self._sealed
54	        self._sealed = True
55	        try:
56	            yield self
57	        finally:
58	            self._sealed = prior
59	
60	    def access(self, claims_source: object) -> object:
61	        """Return ``claims_source`` only if the guard is not sealed; else raise."""
62	        if self._sealed:
63	            raise FirewallViolation(
64	                "Firewall A: external claims accessed during blind baseline construction"
65	            )
66	        return claims_source
67	
68	
69	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
70	class DiscoverConfirmSplit:
71	    """Firewall B. Disjoint discovery / confirmation id partitions for unit-level claims."""
72	
73	    discovery_ids: frozenset[str]
74	    confirm_ids: frozenset[str]
75	
76	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
77	        overlap = self.discovery_ids & self.confirm_ids
78	        if overlap:
79	            raise FirewallViolation(
80	                f"Firewall B: discovery and confirmation partitions overlap on "
81	                f"{len(overlap)} id(s), e.g. {sorted(overlap)[:3]}"
82	            )
83	
84	    @classmethod
85	    def from_partition(
86	        cls, discovery_ids: Iterable[str], confirm_ids: Iterable[str]
87	    ) -> "DiscoverConfirmSplit":
88	        return cls(frozenset(discovery_ids), frozenset(confirm_ids))
89	
90	    def assert_confirm_only(self, ids: Iterable[str]) -> None:
91	        """Raise if any of ``ids`` was seen during discovery (i.e. would double-dip)."""
92	        ids = frozenset(ids)
93	        leaked = ids & self.discovery_ids
94	        if leaked:
95	            raise FirewallViolation(
96	                f"Firewall B: confirmatory test touched {len(leaked)} discovery id(s) — "
97	                f"double-dipping, e.g. {sorted(leaked)[:3]}"
98	            )
99	        unknown = ids - self.confirm_ids
100	        if unknown:
101	            raise FirewallViolation(
102	                f"Firewall B: confirmatory test used {len(unknown)} id(s) outside the "
103	                f"confirmation partition, e.g. {sorted(unknown)[:3]}"
104	            )
105

1	"""Confirmatory-test machinery (METHODOLOGY.md §2, §4).
2	
3	A confirmatory test takes a *locked* hypothesis and returns a :class:`Verdict`. Two
4	mechanisms, selected by hypothesis kind:
5	
6	- unit-level → evaluate on a held-out confirmation partition (Firewall B via
7	  :class:`DiscoverConfirmSplit`);
8	- whole-corpus → compare the observed statistic against a null/permutation distribution and
9	  require stability across resamples.
10	
11	This module defines the *contract and guards* around confirmation. The concrete statistics
12	are supplied by callers via the baseline toolkit; the engine's responsibility here is to
13	refuse to confirm an unlocked hypothesis or on discovery data, and to map an outcome onto
14	one of the three verdicts honestly (including ``indeterminate``).
15	"""
16	
17	from __future__ import annotations
18	
19	from typing import Iterable
20	
21	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import DiscoverConfirmSplit, FirewallViolation
22	from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Hypothesis, HypothesisKind
23	from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import Verdict, VerdictLabel
24	
25	
26	def require_locked(hypothesis: Hypothesis) -> None:
27	    """Gate: a hypothesis must be locked + timestamped before any confirmatory step."""
28	    if not hypothesis.locked:
29	        raise FirewallViolation(
30	            f"hypothesis {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} is not locked; confirm is forbidden "
31	            "before pre-registration (Firewall B / pre-registration)"
32	        )
33	
34	
35	def verdict_from_pvalue(
36	    hypothesis_id: str,
37	    *,
38	    statistic: float,
39	    p_value: float,
40	    alpha: float,
41	    powered: bool,
42	    direction_supports_claim: bool,
43	) -> Verdict:
44	    """Map a confirmatory statistic onto the three verdicts.
45	
46	    - not ``powered`` → ``indeterminate`` (the method cannot decide).
47	    - significant and direction agrees with the claim → ``supported``.
48	    - significant and direction disagrees → ``contradicted``.
49	    - not significant → ``indeterminate`` (absence of evidence is not contradiction here;
50	      a stronger contradiction needs an equivalence/severity test, handled by callers).
51	    """
52	    rule = f"two-sided test at alpha={alpha}; powered={powered}"
53	    common = dict(statistic=statistic, threshold=alpha, evidence={"p_value": p_value})
54	    if not powered:
55	        return Verdict.indeterminate(hypothesis_id, rule, notes="underpowered", **common)
56	    if p_value <= alpha:
57	        if direction_supports_claim:
58	            return Verdict.supported(hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
59	        return Verdict.contradicted(hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
60	    return Verdict.indeterminate(
61	        hypothesis_id, rule, notes="not significant at alpha", **common
62	    )
63	
64	
65	def confirm_unit_level(
66	    hypothesis: Hypothesis,
67	    *,
68	    split: DiscoverConfirmSplit,
69	    evaluated_ids: Iterable[str],
70	    statistic: float,
71	    p_value: float,
72	    alpha: float,
73	    powered: bool,
74	    direction_supports_claim: bool,
75	) -> Verdict:
76	    """Confirm a unit-level hypothesis on the held-out partition only."""
77	    if hypothesis.kind is not HypothesisKind.UNIT_LEVEL:
78	        raise ValueError("confirm_unit_level requires a UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis")
79	    require_locked(hypothesis)
80	    split.assert_confirm_only(evaluated_ids)  # Firewall B
81	    return verdict_from_pvalue(
82	        hypothesis.hypothesis_id,
83	        statistic=statistic,
84	        p_value=p_value,
85	        alpha=alpha,
86	        powered=powered,
87	        direction_supports_claim=direction_supports_claim,
88	    )
89	
90	
91	def confirm_whole_corpus(
92	    hypothesis: Hypothesis,
93	    *,
94	    observed: float,
95	    null_distribution: list[float],
96	    alpha: float,
97	    stable_across_resamples: bool,
98	    direction_supports_claim: bool,
99	) -> Verdict:
100	    """Confirm a whole-corpus hypothesis against a null distribution + stability check.
101	
102	    The pattern must (a) beat the permutation/null distribution at ``alpha`` and (b) be
103	    stable across resamples. Failing stability yields ``indeterminate`` even if the point
104	    estimate beats the null — an unstable effect is not confirmable.
105	    """
106	    if hypothesis.kind is not HypothesisKind.WHOLE_CORPUS:
107	        raise ValueError("confirm_whole_corpus requires a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis")
108	    require_locked(hypothesis)
109	    if not null_distribution:
110	        raise ValueError("a null/permutation distribution is required for whole-corpus confirmation")
111	
112	    n = len(null_distribution)
113	    # one-sided empirical p-value (proportion of null at least as extreme), with +1 smoothing
114	    at_least_as_extreme = sum(1 for v in null_distribution if v >= observed)
115	    p_value = (at_least_as_extreme + 1) / (n + 1)
116	    rule = (
117	        f"empirical p from {n}-sample null at alpha={alpha}; "
118	        "requires stability across resamples"
119	    )
120	    common = dict(
121	        statistic=observed,
122	        threshold=alpha,
123	        evidence={"p_value": p_value, "null_n": n, "stable": stable_across_resamples},
124	    )
125	    if not stable_across_resamples:
126	        return Verdict.indeterminate(
127	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="unstable across resamples", **common
128	        )
129	    if p_value <= alpha:
130	        label = (
131	            VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED if direction_supports_claim else VerdictLabel.CONTRADICTED
132	        )
133	        return Verdict(
134	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id,
135	            label,
136	            rule,
137	            statistic=observed,
138	            threshold=alpha,
139	            evidence={"p_value": p_value, "null_n": n, "stable": stable_across_resamples},
140	        )
141	    return Verdict.indeterminate(
142	        hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="does not beat null at alpha", **common
143	    )
144

content.text: Now let me run the repros to verify both claims.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && .venv/bin/python -c '
from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import DiscoverConfirmSplit, FirewallViolation

# Repro 1: empty confirm partition constructs without error
s = DiscoverConfirmSplit.from_partition({"a"}, set())
print("Repro1: constructed OK ->", s)

# Repro 2: assert_confirm_only(set()) passes vacuously
try:
    s.assert_confirm_only(set())
    print("Repro2: assert_confirm_only(empty) passed (NO raise)")
except FirewallViolation as e:
    print("Repro2: raised ->", e)

# Also: a split with non-empty confirm but evaluating empty
s2 = DiscoverConfirmSplit.from_partition({"a"}, {"b","c"})
try:
    s2.assert_confirm_only(set())
    print("Repro2b: assert_confirm_only(empty) passed on non-empty confirm partition (NO raise)")
except FirewallViolation as e:
    print("Repro2b: raised ->", e)
'

content.content:
Repro1: constructed OK -> DiscoverConfirmSplit(discovery_ids=frozenset({'a'}), confirm_ids=frozenset())
Repro2: assert_confirm_only(empty) passed (NO raise)
Repro2b: assert_confirm_only(empty) passed on non-empty confirm partition (NO raise)

content.text: Both repros confirmed. Now let me check the end-to-end claim: can `confirm_unit_level` emit a verdict with empty evaluated_ids? Let me look at the Hypothesis/Verdict types and find callers.

content.content:
1	"""Typed, falsifiable hypotheses (METHODOLOGY.md §3).
2	
3	Every hypothesis names what is claimed, the test, the data it runs on, and a pre-specified
4	decision rule. Two origins:
5	
6	- ``DISCOVERY`` — surfaced by the data, then made specific, locked, and timestamped before
7	  confirmation (Firewall B).
8	- ``EXTERNAL_CLAIM`` — derived by the adapter from an external expert claim, tested against
9	  the blind baseline (Firewall A).
10	
11	Two kinds, which select the confirmation mechanism:
12	
13	- ``UNIT_LEVEL`` — confirmed on a held-out split.
14	- ``WHOLE_CORPUS`` — no held-out object possible; confirmed against null/permutation
15	  distributions and stability across resamples.
16	"""
17	
18	from __future__ import annotations
19	
20	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
21	from enum import Enum
22	from typing import Any, Mapping
23	
24	
25	class HypothesisKind(Enum):
26	    UNIT_LEVEL = "unit_level"
27	    WHOLE_CORPUS = "whole_corpus"
28	
29	
30	class HypothesisOrigin(Enum):
31	    DISCOVERY = "discovery"
32	    EXTERNAL_CLAIM = "external_claim"
33	
34	
35	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
36	class Hypothesis:
37	    """A locked, falsifiable assertion ready for confirmatory testing.
38	
39	    ``locked_at`` and ``timestamp_proof`` are populated when the hypothesis is sealed for
40	    pre-registration (RFC-3161 token reference). An unlocked hypothesis (both ``None``) must
41	    not be passed to a confirmatory test — the engine enforces this at the gate, and the
42	    ``locked`` property is the in-code check.
43	    """
44	
45	    hypothesis_id: str
46	    statement: str
47	    kind: HypothesisKind
48	    origin: HypothesisOrigin
49	    test_name: str
50	    decision_rule: str
51	    source_claim_id: str | None = None
52	    locked_at: str | None = None
53	    timestamp_proof: str | None = None
54	    params: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
55	
56	    @property
57	    def locked(self) -> bool:
58	        return self.locked_at is not None and self.timestamp_proof is not None
59	
60	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
61	        if self.origin is HypothesisOrigin.EXTERNAL_CLAIM and not self.source_claim_id:
62	            raise ValueError("EXTERNAL_CLAIM hypotheses must carry a source_claim_id")
63

content.text: Let me find the test files for these firewalls and confirm machinery, and check the methodology docs.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && find tests -name "*firewall*" -o -name "*confirm*" 2>/dev/null; echo "---"; grep -rln "confirm_unit_level\|DiscoverConfirmSplit\|assert_confirm_only" tests 2>/dev/null

content.content:
1	"""Confirmatory machinery: locking gate, three-verdict mapping, whole-corpus null test."""
2	
3	import pytest
4	
5	from assay_engine.methodology.confirm import (
6	    confirm_whole_corpus,
7	    require_locked,
8	    verdict_from_pvalue,
9	)
10	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import FirewallViolation
11	from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import (
12	    Hypothesis,
13	    HypothesisKind,
14	    HypothesisOrigin,
15	)
16	from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import VerdictLabel
17	
18	
19	def _locked_whole_corpus() -> Hypothesis:
20	    return Hypothesis(
21	        hypothesis_id="h1",
22	        statement="global structure exceeds chance",
23	        kind=HypothesisKind.WHOLE_CORPUS,
24	        origin=HypothesisOrigin.DISCOVERY,
25	        test_name="permutation",
26	        decision_rule="empirical p <= 0.05 and stable",
27	        locked_at="2026-06-15T00:00:00Z",
28	        timestamp_proof="rfc3161:deadbeef",
29	    )
30	
31	
32	def test_require_locked_blocks_unlocked_hypothesis():
33	    h = Hypothesis(
34	        hypothesis_id="h0",
35	        statement="x",
36	        kind=HypothesisKind.WHOLE_CORPUS,
37	        origin=HypothesisOrigin.DISCOVERY,
38	        test_name="permutation",
39	        decision_rule="p<=.05",
40	    )
41	    assert not h.locked
42	    with pytest.raises(FirewallViolation):
43	        require_locked(h)
44	
45	
46	def test_verdict_mapping_covers_three_outcomes():
47	    supported = verdict_from_pvalue(
48	        "h", statistic=3.0, p_value=0.01, alpha=0.05, powered=True,
49	        direction_supports_claim=True,
50	    )
51	    contradicted = verdict_from_pvalue(
52	        "h", statistic=3.0, p_value=0.01, alpha=0.05, powered=True,
53	        direction_supports_claim=False,
54	    )
55	    underpowered = verdict_from_pvalue(
56	        "h", statistic=0.1, p_value=0.4, alpha=0.05, powered=False,
57	        direction_supports_claim=True,
58	    )
59	    not_sig = verdict_from_pvalue(
60	        "h", statistic=0.1, p_value=0.4, alpha=0.05, powered=True,
61	        direction_supports_claim=True,
62	    )
63	    assert supported.label is VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED
64	    assert contradicted.label is VerdictLabel.CONTRADICTED
65	    assert underpowered.label is VerdictLabel.INDETERMINATE
66	    assert not_sig.label is VerdictLabel.INDETERMINATE
67	
68	
69	def test_whole_corpus_unstable_is_indeterminate_even_if_beats_null():
70	    h = _locked_whole_corpus()
71	    null = [0.0] * 100
72	    v = confirm_whole_corpus(
73	        h, observed=10.0, null_distribution=null, alpha=0.05,
74	        stable_across_resamples=False, direction_supports_claim=True,
75	    )
76	    assert v.is_indeterminate
77	
78	
79	def test_whole_corpus_supported_when_beats_null_and_stable():
80	    h = _locked_whole_corpus()
81	    null = [0.0] * 100
82	    v = confirm_whole_corpus(
83	        h, observed=10.0, null_distribution=null, alpha=0.05,
84	        stable_across_resamples=True, direction_supports_claim=True,
85	    )
86	    assert v.label is VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED
87	
88	
89	def test_whole_corpus_requires_null_distribution():
90	    h = _locked_whole_corpus()
91	    with pytest.raises(ValueError):
92	        confirm_whole_corpus(
93	            h, observed=1.0, null_distribution=[], alpha=0.05,
94	            stable_across_resamples=True, direction_supports_claim=True,
95	        )
96

1	"""Firewalls are enforced in code, not prose (ADR-0005). These tests are the enforcement."""
2	
3	import pytest
4	
5	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import (
6	    ClaimBlindGuard,
7	    DiscoverConfirmSplit,
8	    FirewallViolation,
9	)
10	
11	
12	def test_claim_blind_guard_blocks_access_while_sealed():
13	    guard = ClaimBlindGuard()
14	    claims = object()
15	    with guard.sealed():
16	        assert guard.is_sealed
17	        with pytest.raises(FirewallViolation):
18	            guard.access(claims)
19	    # released after the block — adjudication may now read claims
20	    assert guard.access(claims) is claims
21	
22	
23	def test_claim_blind_guard_restores_prior_state_on_nesting():
24	    guard = ClaimBlindGuard()
25	    with guard.sealed():
26	        with guard.sealed():
27	            assert guard.is_sealed
28	        assert guard.is_sealed  # inner exit must not unseal the outer block
29	    assert not guard.is_sealed
30	
31	
32	def test_split_rejects_overlapping_partitions():
33	    with pytest.raises(FirewallViolation):
34	        DiscoverConfirmSplit.from_partition({"a", "b"}, {"b", "c"})
35	
36	
37	def test_confirm_only_rejects_discovery_ids():
38	    split = DiscoverConfirmSplit.from_partition({"a", "b"}, {"c", "d"})
39	    with pytest.raises(FirewallViolation):
40	        split.assert_confirm_only({"a"})  # double-dipping on discovery data
41	
42	
43	def test_confirm_only_rejects_ids_outside_confirmation_partition():
44	    split = DiscoverConfirmSplit.from_partition({"a"}, {"c", "d"})
45	    with pytest.raises(FirewallViolation):
46	        split.assert_confirm_only({"z"})
47	
48	
49	def test_confirm_only_accepts_held_out_ids():
50	    split = DiscoverConfirmSplit.from_partition({"a", "b"}, {"c", "d"})
51	    split.assert_confirm_only({"c", "d"})  # no raise
52

content.text: Now let me verify the full end-to-end claim that `confirm_unit_level` can emit a verdict with zero held-out objects evaluated.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && .venv/bin/python -c '
from assay_engine.methodology.confirm import confirm_unit_level
from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import DiscoverConfirmSplit
from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Hypothesis, HypothesisKind, HypothesisOrigin

h = Hypothesis(
    hypothesis_id="u1", statement="unit claim",
    kind=HypothesisKind.UNIT_LEVEL, origin=HypothesisOrigin.DISCOVERY,
    test_name="t", decision_rule="p<=.05",
    locked_at="2026-06-15T00:00:00Z", timestamp_proof="rfc3161:beef",
)
# Degenerate: empty confirm partition, evaluate nothing -> verdict emitted
split = DiscoverConfirmSplit.from_partition({"a"}, set())
v = confirm_unit_level(
    h, split=split, evaluated_ids=set(),
    statistic=3.0, p_value=0.001, alpha=0.05, powered=True,
    direction_supports_claim=True,
)
print("VERDICT EMITTED on zero held-out objects ->", v.label)

# Even with a populated confirm partition, evaluating empty still emits:
split2 = DiscoverConfirmSplit.from_partition({"a"}, {"b","c"})
v2 = confirm_unit_level(
    h, split=split2, evaluated_ids=[],
    statistic=3.0, p_value=0.001, alpha=0.05, powered=True,
    direction_supports_claim=True,
)
print("VERDICT EMITTED evaluating empty subset of nonempty partition ->", v2.label)
'

content.content:
VERDICT EMITTED on zero held-out objects -> VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED
VERDICT EMITTED evaluating empty subset of nonempty partition -> VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED

content.text:
The full end-to-end claim is confirmed. A `SUPPORTED` verdict is emitted having evaluated zero held-out objects. Note the second case is actually more damning: even a legitimately-constructed non-empty confirm partition emits a verdict when `evaluated_ids` is empty — the bug is not limited to the empty-partition construction.

Let me check whether `p_value`/`powered` are computed independently (so the caller is "responsible") or whether the engine could plausibly be handed these by an honest-but-buggy upstream. Let me check the methodology docs for the intended contract.

37	**Firewall B — discover / confirm separation.** A pattern used to *discover* a hypothesis
38	must not be the data used to *confirm* it. Discovering a pattern and then "testing" it on
39	the same data proves nothing. So:
40	
41	- discover on an exploration partition (or via descriptive analysis), lock the now-specific
42	  claim, then
43	- confirm on data the discovery never touched.
44	
45	For unit-level claims this is a **held-out split**. For whole-corpus claims (where you
46	cannot hold out part of the object — e.g. global structure or coverage), confirmation is
47	against **null / permutation distributions** and **stability across resamples**: the
48	pattern must beat chance on something it was not fit to. Same principle, different
49	mechanism.
50	
51	## 3. Hypotheses
52	
53	Every hypothesis is **typed and falsifiable** — it names what is claimed, the test, the
54	data it runs on, and a pre-specified decision rule.
55	
56	- **Discovery mode:** the data surfaces candidate hypotheses; they are made specific,
57	  **locked, and timestamped before confirmation** (see Firewall B). The questions are
58	  data-derived, which is legitimate *because* discovery and confirmation use different
59	  data — not because the questions were guessed in advance.
60	- **Adjudicate-external-claims mode:** each external claim is itself a pre-stated,
61	  falsifiable assertion about the data. The adapter converts each into a typed hypothesis.
62	  These are tested against the blind baseline.
63	
64	"The claim" and "whether the claim is correct" are kept as separate objects throughout.
65	
66	## 4. Verdicts
67	
68	Confirmatory tests return one of **three** verdicts — never a forced binary:
69	
70	- **Supported** — the baseline corroborates the claim/hypothesis at the pre-specified
71	  threshold.
72	- **Contradicted** — the baseline is inconsistent with the claim at the pre-specified
73	  threshold.
74	- **Indeterminate** — the method cannot decide: underpowered, out of the measurement's
75	  reach, or the disagreement is plausibly a limitation of the method rather than of the
76	  claim. Indeterminate is a real outcome, structured like the others — not a failure.
77	
78	The third verdict is deliberate. A measure disagreeing with a claim is not automatically
79	proof the claim is wrong; it may be a limit of what the baseline can see. `indeterminate`
80	is the honest home for that case.
81

content.text:
Now let me note the comparison with `confirm_whole_corpus`, which explicitly guards against the degenerate empty case (`if not null_distribution: raise ValueError`). This is the parallel mechanism and establishes the intended pattern: the engine refuses to produce a verdict from an empty evidence base. The unit-level path lacks the equivalent guard.

Let me run the existing tests to confirm baseline state.

All evidence is gathered. Let me assess the counter-arguments.

**Counter-argument 1: Is an empty confirm partition ever legitimate?** No. The whole point of unit-level confirmation is a held-out split. An empty confirm partition means there is nothing to hold out / nothing to test on. The methodology explicitly routes the "cannot hold out" case to `confirm_whole_corpus` instead. So an empty confirm partition for a UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis is always nonsensical.

**Counter-argument 2: Is requiring non-empty `evaluated_ids` the right place to enforce?** This is the strongest refutation attempt. One could argue the caller is responsible for computing `statistic`/`p_value`/`powered` from real data, and `powered=True` is the caller's lie. But: (a) the parallel `confirm_whole_corpus` does enforce a non-empty evidence base (`null_distribution`) inside the engine itself — so the engine's stated design philosophy ("firewalls enforced in code, not prose," ADR-0005) demands the same here; (b) `assert_confirm_only` is explicitly Firewall B's runtime enforcement, and a Firewall that passes vacuously on empty input is not enforcing anything. The asymmetry between the two confirm paths is itself the bug.

**Severity:** MEDIUM is appropriate. It is not a silent data-corruption or security issue, and it requires an empty/degenerate input to trigger (an honest pipeline with a real split would not hit it). But it defeats a core methodological guarantee (Firewall B), can emit a falsely `SUPPORTED` verdict with zero held-out evidence, and the project's entire value proposition is that these firewalls are enforced in code. That is squarely MEDIUM, not low.

VERDICT: TRUE_POSITIVE
CONFIDENCE: high
SEVERITY_ASSESSMENT: MEDIUM is correct. Not high (no security/silent-corruption vector; requires a degenerate empty input that an honest split would not produce), but legitimately medium: it defeats Firewall B's runtime enforcement and lets `confirm_unit_level` return a non-indeterminate verdict (e.g. SUPPORTED) backed by zero held-out objects, in a codebase whose stated thesis (ADR-0005) is that firewalls are enforced in code, not prose.

REASONING:
- Repro 1 confirmed: `DiscoverConfirmSplit.from_partition({"a"}, set())` constructs without error. `__post_init__` (firewalls.py:76-82) only checks for discovery/confirm overlap, never for an empty confirm partition.
- Repro 2 confirmed: `assert_confirm_only(set())` does not raise. Both guards in the method (`leaked = ids & discovery_ids`, `unknown = ids - confirm_ids`) are empty-set operations that are vacuously satisfied when `ids` is empty (firewalls.py:90-104).
- End-to-end confirmed: `confirm_unit_level(locked UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis, split, evaluated_ids=set(), p_value=0.001, powered=True, ...)` returns `VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED`. The finding's core claim — a verdict emitted for a held-out test that touched zero held-out objects — reproduces exactly.
- Stronger than the finding states: the defect is not confined to the empty-partition construction. With a legitimately non-empty confirm partition `{"b","c"}`, passing `evaluated_ids=[]` still returns SUPPORTED. So requiring non-empty `confirm_ids` at construction would not fully close the hole; the empty `evaluated_ids` path must also be rejected.
- Design-asymmetry evidence: the parallel `confirm_whole_corpus` (confirm.py:109-110) explicitly raises `ValueError` when its evidence base (`null_distribution`) is empty — the unit-level path has no equivalent non-empty-evidence guard, which is the inconsistency the finding identifies.
- Counter-arguments weighed and rejected: an empty held-out split is never legitimate for a UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis (the "cannot hold out" case is routed to whole-corpus by design, METHODOLOGY.md §2); and "the caller owns the statistic" does not excuse the engine, because Firewall B is explicitly a runtime primitive (ADR-0005) and the sibling path already enforces its evidence base in code.

FIX_GUIDANCE: Enforce non-emptiness at two points. (1) In `DiscoverConfirmSplit.__post_init__`, raise `FirewallViolation` if `confirm_ids` is empty — a unit-level held-out split with nothing held out is invalid by construction (and route the genuine no-holdout case to whole-corpus). (2) More importantly, in `assert_confirm_only` (or in `confirm_unit_level` before it calls into the split), raise `FirewallViolation` when `frozenset(ids)` is empty, so a confirmatory test that evaluated zero held-out objects cannot pass the firewall — mirroring the existing `if not null_distribution: raise` guard in `confirm_whole_corpus`. Add regression tests asserting both the empty-partition construction and the empty-`evaluated_ids` confirm now raise.
